metadata.title: Same Average Size, Different Product
metadata.subtitle: Why Nanoparticle Batches Can't Be Proved Identical Like Pills
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Nanomedicine card 17. Source chapter: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/11-characterization-manufacturing-and-regulatory-translation.md. Manim move: compare. One concept only: mean does not equal distribution, and the spread defines the product.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, teal = narrow / controlled / monodisperse, crimson = wide / heterogeneous / high-PDI, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = narrow distribution / controlled / monodisperse / low PDI; CRIMSON #BF3339 = wide distribution / heterogeneous / high PDI / the product problem; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO TEM artifact discussion, NO GMP facilities tour, NO olaratumab regulatory path, NO full characterization cascade, NO encapsulation efficiency parameters. One concept only: mean does not equal distribution, and the spread defines the product.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Two injectable nanoparticles. Same cancer drug. Same lab. Both labeled one hundred nanometers. A regulator looks at the data and says they are not the same product.

card.sub: Why Batches Can't Be Proved Identical Like Pills

beats.narration_text: A clinical-grade liposomal batch clears from a patient's bloodstream in forty-five minutes. The reference batch circulated for six hours. Both are labeled one hundred nanometers. Something other than the average diameter is driving the difference.

props.headline: A clinical-grade liposomal batch clears from a patient's bloodstream in forty-five…

beats.scene_description: a close-up editorial illustration of two nearly-identical amber vials side by side, both labeled '100 nm', on aged newsprint ground, flat print treatment, desaturated, one vial subtly shadowed
beats.image_prompt: B02, two amber glass pharmaceutical vials side by side on an aged newsprint surface, both labeled '100 nm', editorial flat print illustration style, desaturated, charcoal shadows, cream paper ground, scientific documentary aesthetic
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] two vials — same label, different behavior

beats.narration_text: Two batches of the same cancer nanoparticle measure the same average particle size. Regulators say they are not the same product. Why isn't the average enough?

card.copy: Two batches of the same cancer nanoparticle measure the same average particle size. Regulators say they are not the same product.

beats.narration_text: For a small molecule, identity is binary. One defined chemical structure — the molecule is either that structure or it is not. Batch B matches batch A or it does not. One measurement decides.

production_viz.label: small molecule = one point
production_viz.mechanic: a single teal point appears center frame, labeled 'one structure'. A bracket below reads 'identical or not'. Simple and binary.
production_viz.note: establish the naive expectation — binary identity for small molecules

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] one molecule = one point, binary identity

beats.narration_text: A nanoparticle is not a single structure. It is a population — a cloud of objects that differ in size, spread around a mean. The average diameter is one number pulled from that cloud. The cloud itself is the product.

production_viz.label: nanoparticle = a distribution, not a point
production_viz.mechanic: dots of varying sizes appear in a rough bell spread, with an arrow to the mean. The mean is a single vertical tick. The spread is the full cloud.
production_viz.note: the key conceptual shift — particle as population, not point

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] population cloud — mean is just one tick in the spread

beats.narration_text: Here are two batches. Both have a mean diameter of ninety-eight nanometers. But look at the distributions. Batch A: a tight narrow spike. Batch B: a wide bell, stretched far in both directions. Same center point. Completely different populations.

production_viz.label: same mean, different spread — the core compare
production_viz.mechanic: Two side-by-side histogram columns grow upward. Left column: teal narrow spike with bars clustering tightly at 98 nm. Right column: crimson wide bell spread from 30 to 300 nm. Shared gold vertical tick at 98 nm mean for both. This is the KEY visual.
production_viz.note: THE core compare visual — narrow teal spike vs wide crimson bell, same mean tick

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] side-by-side histograms — teal narrow vs crimson wide, same gold mean tick

beats.narration_text: The number that captures this spread is called the polydispersity index, or PDI. A PDI near zero means the distribution is narrow and controlled — monodisperse. A PDI above roughly zero-point-two flags problematic heterogeneity.

production_viz.label: PDI scale — 0.07 teal vs 0.31 crimson
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal axis labeled PDI. A teal chip at 0.07 with label 'monodisperse / controlled'. A crimson chip at 0.31 with label 'heterogeneous / high PDI'. A threshold marker at 0.2 labeled 'problem threshold'.
production_viz.note: introduce PDI as a concept; threshold approx 0.2 from chapter

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] PDI number line — teal 0.07 vs crimson 0.31

beats.narration_text: A high-PDI batch is not one product. It is three products blended together: small particles that clear from the bloodstream in minutes, medium particles that behave as designed, and large particles that are immediately swallowed by the liver and spleen.

production_viz.label: three populations inside one high-PDI batch
production_viz.mechanic: The wide crimson histogram from B06 returns. Three bracket regions are annotated: left tail 'small: rapid clearance', center 'mid-range: designed behavior', right tail 'large: liver / spleen'. Each region gets a label chip.
production_viz.note: reveal that wide distribution = three pharmacokinetic profiles

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three annotated regions in the wide distribution

beats.narration_text: Those three populations have different pharmacokinetics — different rates of clearance, distribution, and arrival at the tumor. When you report one average number, you average away all three behaviors. The average diameter matches. The drug delivery does not.

document.quote: Two batches with identical mean size and different PDIs are not the same product.
document.attribution: — NCI Nanotechnology Characterization Laboratory framework principle

beats.new_visual_element: [QUOTE] identical mean does not equal same product

beats.narration_text: Matching the mean matches only the center point of a distribution. Matching the distribution matches the whole population — the circulation time, the clearance rate, the fraction that reaches the tumor. The distribution is the whole product.

production_viz.label: matching the mean vs matching the distribution
production_viz.mechanic: Two column comparison. Left: two overlapping single ticks — 'match the mean'. Right: two overlapping histogram curves — 'match the distribution'. The right side shows that matching the distribution is the stronger, fuller claim.
production_viz.note: the resolution — the distribution is the product, not the mean

beats.narration_text: An illustrative example. A QC team receives two batches of liposomal doxorubicin. DLS reads ninety-eight nanometers mean for both. Batch A: PDI zero-point-zero-seven — narrow, monodisperse. Batch B: PDI zero-point-three-one — fifteen percent of particles are above two hundred nanometers, cleared by the liver in under thirty minutes. At the same administered dose, patients in the Batch B cohort receive roughly forty percent less drug at the tumor. Both batches carry the same label.

production_viz.label: Batch A PDI 0.07 vs Batch B PDI 0.31 — illustrative
production_viz.mechanic: Side-by-side two-column layout. Left: Batch A — teal narrow spike, PDI 0.07, label 'tumor delivery: 100%'. Right: Batch B — crimson wide bell, PDI 0.31, label 'tumor delivery: ~60%'. Both share the 98 nm mean tick. A ILLUSTRATIVE chip at bottom.
production_viz.note: THE EXAMPLE act — all numbers invented and labeled illustrative

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Batch A vs Batch B — illustrative example with delivery gap

beats.narration_text: Why isn't the average enough? Because a nanoparticle is a distribution, not a molecule. The mean is the center of a spread — and a high polydispersity index means that spread is wide, containing three different pharmacokinetic populations, all averaged away by one number. Matching the distribution is what proves the product.

card.copy: A nanoparticle is a distribution, not a molecule.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Cancer Nanomedicine series.
